questions.question: What visual layout does Notion’s Feed view use to display database pages?

options.explanation: Correct – Feed view shows pages as a vertical stack of cards, similar to a blog or social feed.
Wrong – The grid layout belongs to Gallery view, not Feed view.

options.text: A traditional table with rows and columns
options.explanation: Wrong – Table view displays rows and columns; Feed view does not.
Wrong – Calendar view shows items on a date grid; Feed view is not a calendar.

questions.question: Which interaction is NOT mentioned as a built‑in feature of Feed view?

options.explanation: Scrolling is a core feature of Feed view.
Commenting is explicitly supported.
View tracking is listed as a feature.

options.text: Editing property values inline from the feed
options.explanation: Correct – Feed view does not allow inline editing of property values; you must open the page.

questions.question: When adding a Feed view to an existing database, what is the first action you must take?

options.text: Click the "+" button at the top left of the database
options.explanation: Correct – The "+" opens the view selector where Feed can be chosen.
Wrong – That shortcut creates a feed on a new page, not within an existing database.
Wrong – Settings does not directly add a new view.

options.text: Duplicate the database and rename it
options.explanation: Wrong – Duplicating is unnecessary for adding a new view.

questions.question: Where can you find the controls to change which properties are visible in a Feed view?

options.text: The View settings icon that looks like sliding scales
options.explanation: Correct – Clicking this icon opens Property visibility where you can toggle 👁️ icons.
Wrong – Right‑clicking the title does not expose property visibility options.

options.text: The top‑right three‑dot menu of the page
options.explanation: Wrong – That menu contains page‑level actions, not view‑specific property toggles.

options.text: The property bar at the bottom of the screen
options.explanation: Wrong – The property bar shows selected properties but doesn’t let you hide/show them.

questions.question: What exact command should you type to create a Feed view on a brand‑new page?

options.explanation: Correct – The slash command "/Feed view" creates a feed block on a new page.
Wrong – That command does not exist in Notion.
Wrong – "/Add view" is not a valid slash command.
Wrong – The full command includes the word "view".

questions.question: Which of the following use cases is the Feed view especially designed for?

options.explanation: Correct – The article highlights Feed view as ideal for sharing updates and status information.

options.text: Tracking detailed project deadlines on a timeline
options.explanation: Wrong – Timeline view is better suited for deadline tracking.

options.text: Performing complex data analysis with filters and formulas
options.explanation: Wrong – While you can filter, Feed view isn’t optimized for deep analysis.

options.text: Managing inventory with multiple numeric properties
options.explanation: Wrong – A table or board view is more appropriate for inventory management.

questions.question: Which statement accurately describes the difference between creating a Feed view on a new page versus adding it to an existing database?

options.text: Both methods require clicking the "+" button in the database toolbar.
options.explanation: Wrong – The new‑page method uses a slash command, not the "+" button.

options.text: Creating on a new page uses the "/Feed view" slash command, while adding to an existing database uses the "+" button to select Feed from the dropdown.
options.explanation: Correct – The article outlines these two distinct workflows.

options.text: Adding to an existing database first requires you to create a brand‑new page.
options.explanation: Wrong – You can add a view directly within the current database without a new page.

options.text: Both approaches automatically hide all properties until you manually show them.
options.explanation: Wrong – Property visibility follows the database’s existing settings; they aren’t hidden by default.

questions.question: When toggling property visibility in a Feed view, which icon indicates that a property is currently visible?

options.explanation: Correct – The eye icon marks a property as visible; clicking it hides the property.
Wrong – The lock icon is not used for visibility in Feed view.
Wrong – The pencil indicates editing, not visibility.
Wrong – The bar chart represents analytics, not property visibility.
